headline: Another federal inmate in New Mexico tests positive for measles
summary: The New Mexico Department of Health reported six measles cases among federal inmates in state detention facilities, with potential public exposure at a Las Cruces courthouse on February 24, prompting 21-day symptom monitoring through March 17 for those present. NMDOH deployed vaccination teams to three county jails and is coordinating protocols with facilities while urging MMR vaccination through public statements. Deputy State Epidemiologist Dr. Chad Smelser emphasized vaccination as the best protection. The department established a bilingual helpline (1-833-SWNURSE) and text service for community inquiries. This outbreak underscores public health challenges in correctional settings requiring coordinated state-federal response.

headline: The Minneapolis council member on the front lines of the resistance to ICE
summary: Federal immigration enforcement under "Operation Metro Surge" maintains approximately 650 agents in Minnesota, conducting raids and deportations that have galvanized local elected officials like Minneapolis City Council Member Jason Chavez, who patrols neighborhoods and coordinates emergency assistance for affected immigrants; the Minneapolis City Council has responded by strengthening its separation ordinance to block police cooperation with ICE, allocating $1 million for rent assistance and $7 million for small business recovery, despite Mayor Jacob Frey's budgetary objections; tensions between progressive council members and the mayor reflect strategic divisions over confronting federal enforcement, with Chavez criticizing Democratic leadership at all levels for insufficient action while personally experiencing family deportations; community members have established encrypted surveillance networks to track ICE movements, and Chavez operates a bilingual information clearinghouse connecting immigrants statewide with legal aid and mutual aid resources.

headline: Indiana board finalizes new A-F school accountability system
summary: Indiana's State Board of Education unanimously adopted a new K-12 accountability system shifting from schoolwide test score averages to student-by-student progress measures incorporating proficiency, growth, and success indicators like literacy and post-graduation readiness; the 2025-26 "Year Zero" will release informational grades only before full implementation in 2026-27, while state officials concurrently pursue a federal waiver to consolidate education funding into a block grant aligned with the new framework.

headline: Indiana treasurer ‘talking to leaders at all levels’ about AES acquisition concerns
summary: Indiana State Treasurer Daniel Elliott, who manages over $16 billion in state funds as the chief investment officer, announced his opposition to a $33 billion utility privatization deal during a State Budget Committee meeting, citing concerns about central Indiana ratepayer affordability and fiscal mismanagement by investors that could indirectly impact the state's financial conditions and economic outlook relevant to budget planning.

headline: Incarcerated individuals boycott digital devices to protest high costs
summary: Nevada prisoners launched a 12-week boycott of ViaPath tablets to protest predatory pricing structures authorized under Assembly Bill 35, pressuring state officials to review NDOC's revenue-sharing contract with the telecommunications provider and highlighting a policy problem where families subsidize underfunded prison services through inflated communication fees.

headline: Wyoming could enact more citizenship checks for SNAP recipients, collect hospital data for noncitizens
summary: Senate File 106 would require Wyoming hospitals to annually report deidentified uncompensated care data for noncitizen and undocumented patients to the state health department, tracking approximately 80 emergency cases per year currently covered by a Medicaid fund; the Wyoming Hospital Association supported the measure after securing confidentiality protections, but warned that data collection could deter individuals from seeking care, potentially undermining public health access.

headline: Immigration arrests spark bills to guarantee K-12 education rights for all Va. students
summary: Virginia lawmakers are advancing legislation to codify the 1982 Plyler v. Doe decision guaranteeing K-12 education access regardless of immigration status, responding to heightened federal immigration enforcement after the Trump administration rescinded Biden-era restrictions on ICE operations near schools. The bills would prohibit school officials from collecting or sharing students' immigration status with law enforcement, except when legally mandated, and require the Virginia Department of Education to develop guidance for school personnel. This state action counters national efforts by conservative groups like The Heritage Foundation urging states to challenge Plyler v. Doe as an economic burden. Federal DHS directives now explicitly permit enforcement in schools, stating criminals "will no longer be able to hide in America's schools...to avoid arrest." The legislation represents a direct policy confrontation between state-level protections and federal immigration enforcement priorities.

headline: Hawaiʻi Lawmakers Push Back On Green’s Plan To Defer Tax Cuts
summary: House Finance Chair Chris Todd's proposal to continue 2024 tax relief will impact Hawaii's revenue forecasts and budget planning; the measure could reduce state income, potentially necessitating program cuts or deficit spending; as a key finance leader's initiative, it carries significant weight in budget negotiations; continuing tax relief reflects a policy trade-off between economic stimulus and fiscal sustainability; this decision will shape resource allocation across state government.

headline: Panic Buttons In Schools: $7 Million Effort Is Delayed A Year
summary: Hawaii's two-year-old state contract to install panic buttons in K-12 schools remains unfulfilled, with DOE campuses still lacking access to the safety technology, exposing critical gaps in government procurement execution, school emergency preparedness policy, and inter-agency implementation accountability.

headline: Blue states push to ban ICE at the polls amid federal voter intimidation fears
summary: Democratic legislators in more than half a dozen states (CA, CT, NM, PA, RI, VA, WA, KS) are advancing bills to prohibit ICE agents from operating within 40-250 feet of polling places and election sites, citing concerns that President Trump may deploy federal immigration enforcement to disrupt midterm elections despite existing federal prohibitions on armed personnel at polls. The measures include enforcement mechanisms allowing local election workers to document incidents and permitting lawsuits by county clerks and voters, while the Trump administration has suggested potential ICE deployment and circulated draft executive orders asserting election powers, though DHS has committed to not targeting polling places in 2026. Republican lawmakers largely oppose these bills as unnecessary, with some characterizing them as fearmongering, while Arizona Republicans have proposed legislation requiring ICE presence at polls, creating a sharp partisan divide over immigration enforcement's role in election security.

headline: State Medicaid budgets will decline by $665 billion under new federal law, report finds
summary: President Trump's One Big Beautiful Bill Act will cut federal Medicaid funding by $665 billion over the next decade, creating a net $86 billion reduction in state budgets as some states must increase general fund spending to cover losses while others save money from reduced enrollment; state impacts vary dramatically, with California and New York facing the largest total budget drops at $112 billion and $63 billion respectively, while Florida sees minimal impact and states like Tennessee, Mississippi, Oklahoma, and Kentucky could achieve over 2% general fund savings.

headline: Holdout Democrats leave WA House support for income tax in doubt
summary: Washington's proposed 9.9% millionaires' income tax would generate an estimated $3 billion annually, with Democratic lawmakers divided over revenue allocation between tax relief and general fund spending. Approximately a dozen Democrats are withholding support due to concerns about proposed budget cuts to the Working Connections Child Care program and are demanding funding increases instead. Gov. Ferguson insists a majority of revenue must fund tax relief for working families and small businesses, creating an impasse that threatens passage before the March 12 session deadline.

headline: Idaho budget committee rejects proposals to partially restore wildfire funding cuts
summary: The Idaho Legislature's Joint Finance-Appropriations Committee rejected two proposals to restore $285,000 in state wildfire protection funding for fiscal years 2026 and 2027, despite warnings from Gov. Brad Little's office that deeper cuts would impair fire response capabilities and reduce seasonal firefighter hiring. The Idaho Department of Lands faces a critical shortfall in its wildfire suppression fund, which holds only $1.2 million against potential annual costs of $70 million, forcing a "credit card" approach where emergency costs will be billed to future legislative sessions. Budget committee members, particularly Rep. James Petzke, expressed concerns about the department's prior allocation of $1 million in firefighter bonuses, which distributed $565,000 to full-time office staff rather than frontline personnel, influencing the funding rejection. The department director warned these cuts will prevent establishing a new eastern Idaho fire protection district, reduce prevention work, and ultimately increase state costs by requiring more expensive federal firefighting assistance. While JFAC may reconsider restoration proposals before adjournment, the department is pursuing alternative dedicated funding through House Bill 511 to increase structure protection surcharges on private forest lands.

headline: Anti-DEI bill for Medicaid health care providers introduced in Idaho Legislature
summary: A proposed Idaho bill banning DEI policies for Medicaid healthcare providers could impact immigrant populations by prohibiting training and practices that address cultural competency and bias, with enforcement authority granted to the attorney general and Idaho Department of Health and Welfare to ensure compliance through provider agreements.

headline: ICE moving toward closing El Paso detention camp, report says
summary: The Camp East Montana detention facility, located on the outskirts of El Paso at Fort Bliss, was seen on Sept. 7, 2025.

headline: Kamala Harris endorses James Talarico for U.S. Senate after supporting his opponent in the primary
summary: Vice President Kamala Harris's keynote at the American Federation of Teachers convention in Houston may relate to potential labor unrest, as teachers' unions have historically organized strikes and protests over education policy and working conditions.

headline: As nation’s worst measles outbreak continues, SC senators OK bill banning vaccine mandates
summary: South Carolina faces the nation's worst measles outbreak with 990 cases since October, 95% among unvaccinated individuals, prompting legislative action on vaccine policy. A Senate panel voted 7-1 to advance a bill prohibiting vaccine mandates for children under 2 and rejected 6-2 a bill eliminating religious exemptions for school measles vaccinations. State epidemiologist Linda Bell reported a 70% increase in measles vaccinations this February compared to last year, while officials brace for potential spring break surge. The Department of Public Health focuses on educating the public about disease symptoms and severe complications like pneumonia and encephalitis to encourage vaccination. The debate reflects broader tensions between medical freedom, parental rights, and community health protection amid COVID-19-era vaccine skepticism.

headline: Kansans on food assistance can’t buy candy, soda beginning in February 2027
summary: Kansas must budget for implementation costs related to new SNAP restrictions on candy and soda effective February 2027, including contractor expenses, retailer and recipient communications, and ongoing monitoring and compliance systems, with the Department for Families and Children responsible for execution following legislative mandate and gubernatorial veto override.

headline: Kansas Senate budget seeks to pry state funding from ‘wine and cheese crowd’
summary: The Kansas Senate approved a $26.8 billion budget for FY2025, representing a 3.4% reduction from the current fiscal year. The bill allocates $5 million increases for special education and university research, $41 million for water projects, and authorizes pay raises for highway patrol and legislative staff while deferring decisions for 34,000 other state workers. Significant cuts include $16 million from K-12 mental health services and halving a proposed Meals on Wheels increase. Senator Joe Claeys successfully redirected $1 million from the arts commission and public broadcasting to fund a disability transportation pilot program. The budget passed 21-19, reflecting partisan divisions over spending priorities and the balance between fiscal restraint and social program funding.

headline: Voters approve a majority of Town Meeting Day school budgets
summary: Vermont voters approved 85 school budgets while rejecting 19, marking two years of calmer budget voting after the historic 2024 taxpayer revolt that saw one-third of budgets fail. Rising uncontrollable costs—healthcare up 35% over three years and property taxes up 40% in five years—continue straining districts where salaries and benefits comprise over 70% of budgets. In response, Gov. Phil Scott signed Act 73 last year to consolidate the state's 119 districts and reform education finance, though consensus remains elusive. Political tensions persist as Scott publicly voted against his own district's budget and demanded legislative reform by session's end, prompting education leaders to accuse him of politicizing public education. Meanwhile, voters in Marlboro and Readsboro closed their elementary schools due to declining enrollment, while Woodstock approved a $111 million bond for new school construction.

headline: SD governor, legislative leaders roll out property tax plan with one week left of session
summary: Governor Rhoden reversed his administration's position to support diverting revenue from a scheduled statewide sales tax increase (4.2% to 4.5%) into a homeowner property tax reduction fund seeded with $56 million in one-time state money, creating a budget tradeoff that reduces discretionary funding available for education, Medicaid providers, and state employees while potentially cutting property taxes by up to 15% statewide through reduced school district levies.

headline: Montana Sen. Sheehy gets in altercation with veteran anti-war protester on Capitol Hill
summary: Anti-war protester Brian McGinnis was forcibly removed from a Senate Armed Services hearing by Capitol Police with physical assistance from Montana Sen. Tim Sheehy, resulting in an apparent broken arm for McGinnis who was protesting U.S. support for Israel. McGinnis faces charges of assaulting officers and resisting arrest, while three officers sustained injuries requiring treatment. The incident highlights Capitol Police policy prohibiting protests inside Congressional buildings and directing demonstrators to designated outdoor areas on Capitol grounds.

headline: Gov. Landry has to convince Senate president to spend more on private school vouchers
summary: Louisiana Gov. Jeff Landry is seeking to double funding for the LA GATOR private school voucher program from $44 million to $88.2 million, which would add 4,000-5,000 students and eventually allow universal eligibility including wealthy families. Senate President Cameron Henry has emerged as the primary obstacle, blocking the expansion for the second consecutive year and questioning the program's effectiveness and fiscal sustainability. Henry is demanding evidence that voucher students perform better academically before approving growth, arguing that rapid doubling of spending is unprecedented and risks repeating past budget crises. The standoff reflects a broader policy debate over whether expanding private school subsidies will reduce public education costs or create new unfunded obligations, with Henry warning that increased voucher spending could divert funds from other priorities like economic development. Landry counters that Henry's caution stems from "PTSD" from previous state financial crises under former governors.

headline: Western Wisconsin residents try to turn back a massive factory farm’s DNR permit
summary: Dozens of Wisconsin residents packed a state office building and two overflow rooms for a contested case hearing challenging the DNR's February 2025 permit approval for Ridge Breeze Dairy's expansion to 6,500 cows, with additional viewers on Zoom. The packed hearing reflects sustained grassroots opposition since 2024 led by Grassroots Organizing Western Wisconsin, which has coordinated activists to "pack public hearings" and connect plaintiffs with legal representation. Opponents frame their fight as part of a broader movement against corporate overreach, linking it to resistance against AI data centers statewide. Public testimony focused on concerns about inadequate manure management plans and potential groundwater contamination from 80 million gallons of annual waste. The hearing before Administrative Law Judge Angela Chaput Foy represents the formal legal process for challenging the permit, with her decision appealable to state circuit court.

headline: Following uproar over jails holding ICE detainees, lawmakers consider tweaking state law
summary: Maine legislators are advancing LD 2058, sponsored by Sen. Rachel Talbot Ross (D-Cumberland), to clarify a 1964 law so that county jails are only mandated to detain individuals on criminal charges with probable cause, explicitly excluding civil immigration violations. The bill emerges from community pressure on Cumberland County Jail to end its contract housing federal detainees and aims to resolve ambiguity cited by jail officials about their legal obligations. While supporters argue the original 1964 law never intended to mandate cooperation with federal immigration authorities, sheriffs express concerns about unintended consequences and maintaining federal funding streams, as the jail receives $150 per day per federal detainee. The proposal represents a compromise between Democratic lawmakers, county sheriffs, the ACLU of Maine, and the Department of Corrections, though Republicans on the committee have questioned its implications for federal authority. The debate intensified after the Trump administration removed all immigration detainees from the facility in January following the sheriff's public criticism of a detention, highlighting the political and financial stakes of the legislation.

headline: Another poll finds Platner leading Mills in U.S. Senate primary
summary: A new Pan Atlantic Research poll shows Democratic Senate candidate Graham Platner leading Gov. Janet Mills 46% to 39% among likely primary voters, reversing December's results where Mills led 47% to 37%. Platner's advantage is driven by strong support among men, voters under 55, and independents (where he leads by 33 points), while Mills maintains strength among older voters and registered Democrats. In general election matchups, Platner leads incumbent Sen. Susan Collins 44% to 40%, while Mills ties Collins at 44%. The survey of 810 voters, conducted Feb. 13-March 2, also reveals significant economic anxiety, with 47% reporting their household is worse off financially than a year ago and 75% citing cost of living as a top state issue. A separate University of New Hampshire poll recently found Platner ahead by an even larger margin of 64% to 26%, suggesting momentum for the outsider candidate despite ongoing controversies.

headline: Nebraska lawmakers reject $50 million proposal to increase tobacco taxes
summary: Nebraska lawmakers rejected Legislative Bill 1124, which would have increased cigarette and vape taxes to generate up to $50 million toward closing a $140 million state budget deficit; the cloture motion failed 31-10, requiring 33 votes. The state's budget shortfall, originally projected at $471 million, was reduced to $125 million (closer to $140 million with corrections) through cuts and cash transfers. The bill's failure leaves few options to fill the gap, with the governor's office warning of potential across-the-board cuts and the Appropriations Committee chair mentioning Medicaid spending reductions as a last resort. Opposition came from progressive senators who called the tax regressive and fiscal conservatives who opposed any tax increase. The defeat highlights political divisions over revenue generation versus spending cuts in addressing Nebraska's budget crisis.

headline: Nebraska lawmakers advance main budget adjustments to floor debate, leaving $125 million deficit
summary: The Nebraska Legislature's Appropriations Committee advanced budget bills LB 1071 and 1072 while addressing a deficit that grew from $471 million to $646 million based on new economic forecasts; committee actions reduced the shortfall by over $500 million through cuts and transfers, including $130 million from the rainy day fund and $11 million from the Nebraska Environmental Trust, but Medicaid payment miscalculations increased the remaining gap to approximately $140 million, requiring a $13.3 million amendment during second-round floor debate before the March 25 deadline.

headline: An income tax was proposed in New Hampshire — and you’ll never guess what happened next
summary: A proposal for a 3% state income tax and new statewide property tax has emerged to address New Hampshire's budget challenges, including surging local property taxes and infrastructure deficits. Republicans maintain anti-tax policies that provide the least state education funding in the country while creating significant budgetary risks. State Democratic leaders have distanced themselves from the income tax proposal, leaving the state's financial situation unresolved as local taxes continue rising.

headline: Arkansas governor proposes 3% increase to state budget
summary: Gov. Sarah Huckabee Sanders proposed a $6.7 billion Arkansas budget for FY2027, increasing spending by 3% ($194.5 million) while projecting a $334 million surplus. The administration plans to allocate $100 million of surplus to a Medicaid Sustainability Fund and $70 million for anticipated growth in the school voucher program, which could reach $379 million total cost. Major spending increases include $53.4 million for state employee pay raises, primarily for corrections officers and troopers, plus additional funds for higher education, drug task forces, and corrections medical costs. To prioritize income tax phase-out, the budget implements a two-category system: Category A covers 93% of agency allocations for mandatory expenses, while Category B contains deferrable one-time costs. This approach aims to limit government growth while funding priorities, though bipartisan concerns exist over escalating voucher program costs.

headline: CT high school students argue for increase in ECS funding formula
summary: Connecticut legislators are considering multiple bills to raise the Education Cost Sharing (ECS) grant formula foundation for the first time since 2013, when the per-student cost estimate was set at $11,525; Senate Bill 7 would phase in increases to $15,500 by 2030 before tying it to inflation, while House Bill 5002 would immediately link the foundation to inflation, and a Republican proposal would raise it to $18,581 by 2031. The stagnant funding has forced districts to increase class sizes, defer infrastructure repairs, and rely more heavily on local property taxes, with some districts facing 7%+ tax increases. Democratic leaders Senate President Looney and Majority Leader Duff support the increases, while Governor Lamont's budget did not include ECS changes, proposing instead a Blue Ribbon Panel to study funding reforms. The funding gap, which students estimate at about $4,500 per student when adjusted for inflation, particularly impacts multi-language learners and support services. Republican leaders also support increasing the foundation but want municipalities to be able to lower property taxes as state funding rises.

headline: Uthmeier to open state-level criminal investigation into Raul Castro
summary: Florida Attorney General James Uthmeier announced the re-opening of a criminal investigation into Raul Castro for ordering the 1996 shoot-down of two Brothers to the Rescue flights that killed four individuals, a move that could potentially spark protests within Florida's large Cuban-American community seeking accountability for the decades-old incident.

headline: ​Amid 3 confirmed measles cases, Colorado public health officials advise vaccination
summary: Colorado's third 2026 measles case involves a second unvaccinated Broomfield High School student, with potential exposures Feb. 24-27. State law requires MMR vaccination for school attendance but allows medical and non-medical exemptions; however, exempted students must stay home at least 21 days if a case occurs. CDPHE recommends vaccination as the most effective protection and provides online resources in English and Spanish, while all health insurance including Medicaid covers the vaccine. Colorado cases rose from six between 2014-2024 to 36 in 2025, reflecting declining vaccination rates that officials attribute to misinformation. The U.S. has exceeded 1,000 measles cases in 2026, threatening its measles-free status since 2000.

headline: Bird flu persists across the country; Iowa logs fifth detection this year
summary: The Iowa Department of Agriculture and Land Stewardship confirmed the state's fifth avian influenza detection in 2026 in a Washington County backyard flock, prompting strengthened biosecurity policies and state-federal coordination. USDA data shows the national outbreak has affected over 200 million birds since 2022, with 11.5 million impacted in the past 30 days. Government protocols require culling entire flocks upon detection to contain the fatal virus. State officials are directing producers to limit wildlife exposure and maintain sanitary conditions through published guidance. As a significant disease outbreak, avian influenza surveillance and response in Iowa involves public health oversight and policy implementation across state and federal agencies.

headline: Republicans push forward with proposal to replace Missouri income tax with sales tax
summary: Missouri's Republican-led House is advancing a constitutional amendment to eliminate the state income tax, which generated about 65% of the state's $13.4 billion general revenue in FY2025, replacing it with an expanded sales tax system. The proposal would reduce the 4.7% top income tax rate by 0.01 percentage points for each additional $20 million in revenue growth, with a maximum 1.6 point annual reduction, reaching 1.4% when sufficient revenue is generated. Democrats warn the plan creates fiscal danger and threatens essential programs currently being cut, while Republicans argue it will make Missouri competitive with no-income-tax states. The legislation passed committee on a party-line 7-3 vote and would give lawmakers three years to implement the new tax structure without requiring another statewide vote. To match current revenue without broadening the tax base would require raising the state sales tax to nearly 13%, including first-time taxes on motor fuel.
